content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 14, 2017

Donna Mooney
Chief Assistant City Attorney
City of Vallejo
555 Santa Clara Street
Vallejo CA 94590

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-146

Dear Ms. Mooney:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

May Vallejo Planning Commissioner Roberto Cortez make, participate in making, or influence a decision on an appeal of the Community and Economic Development Director's decision to approve a Planned Development Unit Plan and Minor Use Permit allowing the reuse of a vacant 26,000-square-foot commercial building in downtown Vallejo for a La Clinica de la Raza health care facility?

CONCLUSION

Yes. A decision concerning the La Clinica de la Raza health care facility does not appear to have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Commissioner Cortez's interest in his business or the leased property.

_________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-146
Page No. 2

FACTS

Commissioner Cortez is the owner and chief executive officer of an engineering firm that
leases space at 425 Virginia Street. On June 19, 2017, the Planning Commission is scheduled to
consider and decide an appeal of the Community and Economic Development Director's decision to
approve a Planned Development Unit Plan and Minor Use Permit regarding the reuse of a vacant
26,000-square-foot commercial building in downtown Vallejo for relocating and expanding an
existing La Clinica de la Raza health care facility (the "Project"). The proposed facility would
provide medical, dental and optical care services Monday through Friday from 8 a.m. to 8 p.m., and
on Saturday from 8:30 am to 5:30 pm. At full operation, the Project proponent anticipates to have
up to 100 full-time employees servicing 220 patient visits per day.

The proposed Project includes extensive interior building renovations and minor exterior
improvements to accommodate the facility. In a telephone call on June 6, 2017, you noted that the
proposed facility, at 415-417 Georgia Street, is adjacent to a city owned public parking lot. You
also stated that, from 2011 to 2017, the property had been used as a space for ceremonies and
events, and had been a J.C Penny's department store until the late 1970s.

The subject commercial building is approximately 240 feet from the property leased by
Commissioner Cortez's engineering firm. A downtown property owners' entity, Central Core
Restoration Corporation ("CCRC") has appealed the decision of the Community and Economic
Development Director, asserting that traffic circulation and increased need for parking related to the
clinic will negatively impact existing businesses and uses in the area. The CCRC's appeal also
asserts that allowing medical uses to dominate a downtown area will harm existing commercial,
retail, office and residential uses, and that further environmental review is necessary.

Commissioner Cortez states that he does not believe that any decisions concerning the
appeal will have an effect on the termination date of his firm's lease of property at 425 Virginia
Street, the potential rental value of the property, the rental value or right to sublease the property,
the actual or legally allowable use of the property, his or his firm's use and enjoyment of the
property, or the value of his engineering firm. In a telephone call on June 5, 2017, you indicated
that Commissioner Cortez's engineering firm has a five-year lease on the Virginia Street property,
with three years remaining on the lease.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87103.) Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103
and include the following:

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)

• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).) An "interest in real property" includes "any leasehold,

File No. A-17-146
Page No. 3

beneficial or ownership interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property
located in the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or
other filer, or his or her immediate family if the fair market value of the interest is two
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." (Section 82033.)

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $470 or
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).)

• A public official's own personal finances, or those of a member of his or her immediate
family. (Section 87103.).)

As the owner and chief executive officer of an engineering firm, Commissioner Cortez has
an interest in the firm as a business entity and as a source of income. He also has an interest in the
engineering firm's real property leasehold interest.

Foreseeability: A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard
for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision.
Under your facts, neither the engineering firm nor the property in which it is located are explicitly
involved in the decision. Regulation 18701(b) provides: "In general, if the financial effect can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably
foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not
subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable."

Materiality: A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of
a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. (Regulation 18700(a).) Different
standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be
material depending on the nature of the interest.

Engineering Firm: A reasonably foreseeable effect on an interest in a business entity,
including any business entity that is a source income, not explicitly involved in the decision is
material "if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's
publicly traded stock, or the value of a privately-held business entity." Examples of decisions with
material effects include decisions that:

File No. A-17-146
Page No. 4

• Authorize, prohibit, regulate, or establish conditions for an activity in which the business
is engaged.

• Increase or decrease competition for the business.

• Increase or decrease the need for the products or services supplied by the business.

• Improve the surrounding neighborhood in a manner that may affect the amount of
business the business receives.

• Decide a location of a major development, entertainment facility, or project, which
would increase or decrease the amount of business the business receives.

• Increase or decrease the tax burden, debt, or financial or legal liability of the business.

(Regulation 18702.1(b).)

In this case, the Project is not of the size or scope that would have far-reaching financial
effects. The Project replaces existing commercial uses and is consistent with comparable properties
within downtown Vallejo, which already includes a mix of retail, commercial, and office uses.
Moreover, the engineering firm is not a retail business and does not appear to rely heavily on foot-
traffic. It is unlikely that the Project will significantly affect client accessibility or the amount of
business the engineering firm receives. For these reasons, we find that a decision on the appeal of
the Project will not have a reasonably foreseeable material on Commissioner Cortez's financial
interest in the engineering firm.

425 Virginia Street Lease: For a real property interest in a lease, Regulation 18705.2(b)
states, with limited exceptions, that the "reasonably foreseeable financial effects of a governmental
decision on any real property in which a governmental official has a leasehold financial interest is
material" if the decision will:

"(1) Change the termination date of the lease;

"(2) Increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property;

"(3) Increase or decrease the rental value of the property, and the official has
a right to sublease the property;

"(4) Change the official's actual or legally allowable use of the real property;

"(5) Impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real property."

Once again, considering the scale of the Project and its consistency with the existing
downtown uses, there is no reason to believe that a decision on the appeal of the Project will have a
reasonably foreseeable material effect on Commissioner Cortez's leasehold interest in 425 Virginia
Street.

File No. A-17-146
Page No. 5


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
Acting General Counsel

[signature]

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:jgl
